title: Marketplace and Instalment Commerce Architecture
executiveSummary: A financial-services business wanted to launch a marketplace selling both its own stock and third-party merchants' products, with instalment purchasing available at checkout. The requirement arrived as commerce, lending, merchant operations, logistics and after-sales servicing bundled together with no boundaries between them. The work was to separate those concerns, keep lending decisions where they legally belong, and define a Phase 1 that could actually launch.
industryContext: Marketplaces are usually described as an e-commerce project. This one was not. The instalment option meant a lending decision sat inside the checkout, made by a third party, with consequences for order confirmation, refunds, servicing and collections. That single feature changes the architecture of everything downstream.
businessModel: Two revenue models running through one storefront: direct resale of the organisation's own stock, and commission on third-party merchant sales. Customers could pay in cash or apply for instalments through an external finance provider at checkout. Additional sales channels — a partner website and physical stores — were part of the ambition but not the launch.
operationalChallenge: Support direct resale and third-party merchant models through a single customer experience
Onboard merchants without inheriting their risk or their data quality
Retain manual control over merchant and catalogue approval to protect the brand
Present cash and instalment options at checkout without making a lending decision internally
Integrate an external finance provider whose decision gates order confirmation
Calculate commission and pay merchants on a trigger everyone agrees to before launch
Assign courier fulfilment across products with very different logistics profiles
Establish who is responsible for repayment, servicing and after-sales — the marketplace, the merchant or the provider
Separate the launch scope from the partner-website and physical-store ambitions

currentState.summary: There was no current state to map in the usual sense. The 'current state' was a requirement: several business models described at once, with ownership boundaries between the marketplace, the merchants and the finance provider left implicit.
steps: Customer checkout with a finance option
risks: Responsibility overlap between marketplace, merchant and provider
Unclear repayment ownership
Scope expansion across channels
Financial reconciliation complexity
Regulatory exposure if lending logic drifted internal
gaps: No defined merchant approval criteria or authority
No refund path for a financed purchase

processGaps: The lending decision and the order confirmation were described as one step, when they are two events owned by two organisations
Payout was described as 'after the sale' — which could mean order, dispatch, delivery or the end of the return window, each with a different financial exposure
Merchant onboarding had no verification standard, so risk was unquantified
Returns on a financed purchase had no path at all — the money involves a third party
After-sales responsibility defaulted to whoever the customer called, which is not a design
keyDiscoveryQuestions: Who makes the lending decision, and are they licensed to make it?
What does the customer see while the finance decision is pending, and what happens on decline?
What event earns a merchant their money — and do you hold payout for the return window?
When a financed order is returned, who refunds whom, and in what order?
After the sale, who does the customer believe they are dealing with — and who is contractually responsible?
Which of these channels must exist on day one, and which are ambitions?

futureState.summary: A phased operating model: controlled merchant onboarding, lending decisions held externally, an explicit order state machine gated on the finance decision, and payout triggered by confirmed delivery after the return window.
steps: Customer checkout with cash or instalment selection
controls: Manual merchant approval retained deliberately
Mandatory logistics data before a product can list
Lending decision external by design
Order confirmation gated on the provider's response
Payout trigger tied to confirmed delivery plus return window
Written responsibility matrix for servicing and collections

solutionArchitecture.summary: A marketplace layer for merchant and catalogue operations, an external finance provider as the lending authority, and the financial platform as the source of truth for money. The commerce system holds state and orchestration; it does not calculate credit and it does not own the ledger.

layers.name: Marketplace / commerce layer
layers.role: Merchant records, catalogue, orders, order state machine, customer experience

layers.name: External finance provider
layers.role: Lending decision, repayment schedule and the repayment relationship with the customer — outside the build

layers.name: Financial platform (ERP / accounting)
layers.role: Source of truth for invoices, credit notes, payouts and reconciliation

layers.name: Support layer
layers.role: Servicing and after-sales tickets, routed by responsibility rather than by channel

layers.name: Logistics integrations
layers.role: Courier assignment, consignment creation, tracking and delivery confirmation

dataFlow: Merchant application → verification → merchant record with commercial terms
Product submission → automated checks → manual approval → published catalogue
Checkout with instalment → application to provider → decision → order state updated
Order confirmed → stock allocated → courier assigned → dispatched → delivery confirmed
Delivery confirmed + return window elapsed → commission calculated → payout instruction → ledger entry in the financial platform
Return raised → inspection → outcome → refund coordinated with the provider where financed

standardVsCustom.reasoning: A status model with approval routing and reason codes; no custom build needed, and keeping it configurable meant criteria could change without development.
This is the provider's product and, in most markets, their licence. Building it internally would have created regulatory exposure for no commercial gain.
Held as rule data per merchant rather than logic in code — commission structures change per merchant and per campaign, and hard-coding them guarantees a change request every quarter.
The financial platform already owned this. Recreating it in the commerce layer would have produced two versions of the truth about money.
Selection depended on dimensions, handling requirements and destination in combinations no standard rule engine expressed — a contained piece of custom logic with a clear boundary.

scopeDecisions.reasoning: Each channel imposes its own order states and fulfilment rules. Adding them before the core model was proven would have made the state machine unfinishable.
Brand risk outweighed throughput at launch. The decision was made explicitly and its cost (a human bottleneck) was documented rather than discovered later.
A boundary, not a feature deferral. Written into the scope so that nobody could add it by increment.
The provider owns the repayment relationship. The marketplace's obligation is routing the customer correctly when they call the wrong party — which they will.

dependencies: Finance provider API access, sandbox and decision-response contract
Signed commercial terms defining commission, payout timing and return liability
Merchant verification standard agreed with compliance
clientResponsibilities: Agree the payout trigger commercially before it is built
Confirm the servicing and collections responsibility split with the provider in writing
Provide merchant verification criteria and name the approving role
Resource manual approval at launch volumes
Confirm refund treatment for financed orders with the provider
risks: Regulatory exposure if lending logic drifted inside the commerce layer by increment
Provider downtime blocking checkout with no defined fallback
Payout before the return window creating unrecoverable exposure
Manual approval becoming the growth ceiling
Customers contacting the wrong party for repayment issues and receiving no answer
contribution: Separated a bundled requirement into commerce, lending, merchant operations, fulfilment and servicing as distinct concerns with owners
Established the lending boundary and wrote it into scope as an exclusion rather than a deferral
Designed the order state machine with the finance decision as an explicit gate
Defined the payout trigger and surfaced its financial exposure to the client as a decision, not an assumption
Modelled merchant onboarding, catalogue approval and the responsibility matrix for after-sales
Phased the channels and documented what each additional channel would cost the state model
Produced the discovery question set, process flows, architecture and Initial System Overview
deliverablesProduced: Scope, assumptions, dependencies and client responsibilities

effect.statement: A launchable Phase 1 with the lending boundary held externally, a payout trigger the business had consciously chosen, and channel ambitions phased rather than abandoned. Outcome measures were not published.

title: Marine Licensing and Relationship Architecture
locationContext: European marine and yacht services business
executiveSummary: A marine services business needed a system to manage licensing and contracts around vessels. The difficulty was not the workflow — it was the relationship model. A single vessel could have six different company roles attached to it at the same time, each capable of changing independently, and each mattering for a different reason. Most of the value of this engagement was in refusing to flatten that.
industryContext: In marine and yacht services, the vessel is the asset, but the vessel is not the customer. Ownership, management, licensing, billing, brokerage and shipyard involvement are separate commercial relationships that can sit against the same vessel simultaneously — and can each change without the others changing.
businessModel: Licensing and management services delivered around vessels, with revenue tied to contracts and licences whose parties are frequently not the same entity that pays, operates or owns the asset.
operationalChallenge: Model a vessel with several simultaneous company roles rather than one 'account'
Track which party holds which role, and for what period
Handle the billing party being a different entity from the owner and the operator
Support brokers and shipyards as participants without treating them as customers
Manage licences and contracts with renewal cycles across multiple currencies and entities
Preserve history: who was responsible for this vessel last year is a real question

currentState.summary: Relationships were held across documents, spreadsheets and institutional memory. The register could describe a vessel, but not reliably answer who was responsible for it, in what capacity, on a given date.
steps: Billing routed by the person who knows
risks: Wrong party invoiced
Renewal missed
No history of role changes
Key-person dependency
Contract terms not extractable as data
gaps: Currency and entity handling ad hoc

processGaps: The single-customer assumption in standard CRM objects could not express six concurrent roles against one asset
Role changes had no effective dates, so the register described now and forgot then
The billing party was inferred rather than recorded, which is an invoicing error waiting to happen
Renewals depended on a person, not a date field
Contract terms lived in prose, so nothing downstream could act on them
keyDiscoveryQuestions: Who can be involved with one vessel, and in what capacity?
Can one company hold more than one role against the same vessel at the same time?
Who receives the invoice, and is that ever a different party from the owner?
What happens to every role when the vessel is sold?
Do you need to know who was responsible last year, and why?
Which of these parties is a customer, and which is a participant?

futureState.summary: A vessel-centric data model with parties attached through dated role relationships, so that responsibility, billing and licensing can each be answered independently — and historically.
steps: Vessel registered as the central asset
Companies registered once, independent of role
Roles attached to the vessel with validity periods
Contract created referencing the relevant parties and their roles
Licence issued against the vessel with a validity period
Renewal cycle triggered from the licence date
Role change recorded as a dated event, not an overwrite
Historical position queryable at any date
controls: Role relationships carry effective and end dates
A company record exists once and holds many roles
Billing party explicit on every contract
Renewal monitoring driven by data, not memory
Role changes append to history rather than replacing it

solutionArchitecture.summary: A relationship-first CRM design: vessel as the central object, company as an independent object, and a dated junction between them carrying the role. Contracts and licences reference the junction, not the company directly.

layers.name: Asset layer
layers.role: Vessel as the central record with identity and specification

layers.name: Party layer
layers.role: Companies and individuals recorded once, independent of the roles they hold

layers.name: Relationship layer
layers.role: Dated role links between party and asset — owner, manager, licensee, billing party, broker, shipyard

layers.name: Agreement layer
layers.role: Contracts and licences referencing the parties in their roles, with validity and renewal data

layers.name: Finance interface
layers.role: Billing directed to the recorded billing party, in the correct entity and currency

dataFlow: Vessel registered → companies linked through dated role relationships
Contract created → references parties by role, not by assumption
Licence issued against vessel → validity period set → renewal cycle scheduled
Role change → new dated relationship appended → prior relationship end-dated
Invoice → routed to the billing party recorded for that period

standardVsCustom.requirement: Multiple concurrent company roles per vessel
standardVsCustom.reasoning: No standard account-contact model expresses this. A dated relationship object was the only design that answered the business's real questions without lying about them.
Standard objects with structured key terms and validity dates were sufficient once the party model was right.
Date-driven notification is standard functionality; the difficulty was never the reminder, it was knowing who to remind.
The accounting platform owns currency, tax and fiscal rules. The CRM records which party bills and in which entity — it does not recreate the ledger.

scopeDecisions.requirement: Flattening the model to one customer per vessel
scopeDecisions.reasoning: It would have made the build faster and the system wrong. The whole point of the engagement was the relationship complexity.

scopeDecisions.requirement: Full historical reconstruction of past roles
scopeDecisions.reasoning: The model supports history from go-live. Back-filling prior years is a data project with its own scope and its own cost.
Participants in the relationship model, not subjects of it. Managing their work is a different system.

dependencies: Confirmed list of role types and whether any are mutually exclusive
Accounting platform entity and currency structure
Existing vessel and company data for migration
Legal confirmation of what the licence register must retain
clientResponsibilities: Confirm the complete role taxonomy — adding a seventh role later changes the model
Confirm which party is billed in each scenario
Cleanse existing vessel and company data
Decide how much history must be migrated
risks: A missing role type discovered after build changes the relationship model
Data migration from documents and spreadsheets is larger than it looks
Users may want a simple 'customer' field and reintroduce ambiguity by habit
Historical accuracy expectations exceeding the data that exists
contribution: Identified that the requirement was a data-modelling problem, not a workflow problem
Designed the dated party-role relationship model that allowed six simultaneous roles per vessel
Refused the flattened single-customer design and documented why
Separated the party layer from the role layer so a company is recorded once and holds many roles
Defined billing-party routing explicitly rather than by inference
Kept currency, tax and ledger concerns in the accounting platform
Produced the client-facing Initial System Overview against the partner's document standard
deliverablesProduced: Initial System Overview in the partner's template

effect.statement: A data model that can answer who is responsible for a vessel, in what capacity, at any date — and a licensing register that does not depend on the person who remembers. Outcome measures were not published.

title: CRM Over an Existing ERP: Deciding the Source of Truth
locationContext: South African B2B laboratory and instrumentation distributor
executiveSummary: A B2B distributor of laboratory supplies and scientific instruments had working inventory and accounting platforms and no visibility of its sales pipeline. The temptation was to replace things. The correct answer was a CRM layer that adds the sales process without touching either system of record — which made 'who owns the customer?' the central architectural question of the engagement.
industryContext: Technical distribution runs on a large SKU catalogue, margin discipline and long relationships with a small number of buyers. Quoting requires live-ish stock and price; the operational systems already hold both. What the business lacked was everything between an enquiry arriving and a quote being produced.
businessModel: B2B reseller and distributor supplying laboratory consumables, chemicals and instruments to food and beverage, water, mining, pharmaceutical and manufacturing customers. Roughly a dozen users across sales, management and external sales, with expansion expected.
operationalChallenge: No pipeline visibility — management could not see what sales was working on
No follow-up structure, so opportunities depended on individual memory
Reporting assembled manually from spreadsheets
Quoting took around half an hour against an aspiration of a few minutes
Enquiries arrived via a shared mailbox, telephone, messaging and personal email with no single record
Stock and pricing lived in the inventory platform; sales needed to see them without leaving the CRM
Existing platforms were working and were not to be replaced

currentState.summary: Two operational systems doing their jobs well, with the entire sales process happening in inboxes, phone calls and the heads of the people doing it.
steps: Enquiry arrives on one of several channels
Quotation prepared in the inventory platform
Quote approved or lost in the inventory platform
Invoice and payment handled in the accounting platform
risks: Pipeline invisible to management
Opportunities lost to forgotten follow-up
No comparable data across associates
Reporting effort consuming management time
Customer history fragmented across inboxes
gaps: No link between quote, invoice and payment outside the operational systems

processGaps: Nothing recorded the enquiry before it became a quotation, so lead-source and conversion data did not exist
The quotation existed in the inventory platform but the negotiation around it existed nowhere
Won and lost were determined by an action in the inventory platform, which the CRM would need to observe rather than own
Customer master data lived in the inventory platform, so the CRM could not be allowed to create customers
The full lifecycle — deal to delivery to invoice to payment — was not visible in any single place
keyDiscoveryQuestions: Which system produces the official quotation, and who says so?
Where does the master customer record live — and is the CRM allowed to create one?
What does the salesperson do manually that nobody currently records?
Does sales need live stock, or stock as at this morning?
How long does deal-to-cash take today, and who could tell me?
What links a quotation in one system to an invoice in another?
What reporting does management build by hand every month?

futureState.summary: The CRM records the sales process and the timeline; the inventory platform keeps pricing, stock and the official quotation; the accounting platform keeps invoices and payments. Every state a salesperson creates by hand gets time-stamped in the CRM, with references linking the systems.
steps: Enquiry captured as a lead with source
Converted to a deal with mandatory data
Quotation produced in the inventory platform
Quote number and value recorded against the deal
Negotiation state time-stamped in the CRM
Approval or rejection in the inventory platform reflected as won or lost
Invoice raised in the accounting platform
Payment recorded in the accounting platform
Full history retained against the customer in the CRM
controls: Inventory platform remains the pricing and quotation authority
Accounting platform remains the payment authority
CRM never creates a customer master record
SKU as the linking identifier between systems
Every manual action has a corresponding recorded state
Reference numbers mandatory on the deal

solutionArchitecture.summary: A CRM sales layer over two retained systems of record. The architecture question was not which features to build but which system is allowed to be right about what — and the answer had to be written down before anything else could be estimated.

layers.name: CRM (sales layer)
layers.role: Leads, qualification, deals, stages, activity history, timeline, pipeline reporting. Owns nothing financial.

layers.name: Inventory / operations platform
layers.role: Master customer record, SKU catalogue, stock, pricing, margin and the official quotation. Retained.

layers.name: Accounting platform
layers.role: Invoices, payments and the financial ledger. Retained. Payments recorded here only.

layers.name: Integration layer
layers.role: Reference and state synchronisation between the systems, at a frequency chosen against real API constraints

dataFlow: Enquiry (mailbox, phone, messaging) → lead in CRM with source
Deal in CRM → quotation created in the inventory platform → quote number and value written back to the deal
Approval action in the inventory platform → deal marked won in CRM; rejection → lost with reason
Stock and price surfaced to sales from the inventory platform, refreshed at an agreed interval
Invoice raised in the accounting platform → status reflected against the CRM record, read-only
Payment recorded in the accounting platform → status reflected, never edited from the CRM

standardVsCustom.reasoning: This is what a CRM is for. No custom work was justified.
The inventory platform validates discount, tax, margin and stock and owns the quote number sequence. A second quoting engine would have created two versions of the price.
Customers are created in the inventory platform. Allowing CRM creation would have produced two customer databases within a month.

standardVsCustom.requirement: Stock and price visibility in the CRM
standardVsCustom.reasoning: A read-only surface fed from the inventory platform. The design question was refresh frequency against API limits, not whether to build it.
Reflected read-only from the accounting platform. The client reasonably asked why payment data belonged in the CRM at all — the answer had to be an account-management use case, not a habit.
Requested by the client against a large active catalogue. Contained, and useful precisely because the SKU is the identifier linking the systems.

scopeDecisions.requirement: Replacing the inventory or accounting platform
scopeDecisions.reasoning: Both were working. The problem was the absence of a sales layer, not the presence of operational ones.

scopeDecisions.decision: Scoped against actual API behaviour, not the aspiration
scopeDecisions.reasoning: 'Real time' is a phrase, not a specification. The honest answer was an agreed refresh interval plus on-demand refresh, and saying so early avoided an unwinnable expectation.
The client's own answer on migration was 'uncertain' and referenced a legacy system. Scoping a migration against uncertainty is how estimates fail.
A genuine ambition, and a different process family. Including it would have delayed the visibility the business needed now.

dependencies: API access to the inventory platform, including a separate trial environment for development
Confirmation of the SKU convention and catalogue integrity
Agreement on which reports management actually needs
clientResponsibilities: Confirm that the inventory platform remains the quotation and customer authority
Define what 'handover' and 'follow-up' mean in their own operation — questions the client rightly turned back on the consultant
Decide the acceptable stock refresh interval
Resolve the historical data question
Make sales associates available to validate the stage model
risks: Sales continuing to quote outside the CRM because it remains faster
Two systems drifting toward both owning the customer
API rate limits making the promised freshness undeliverable
Reflected financial data raising access questions nobody had considered
Migration scope expanding once the legacy data is actually examined
contribution: Reframed the request from 'implement a CRM' to 'decide what each system is allowed to be right about'
Established the source-of-truth model across three platforms and wrote it into the client-facing document
Designed the sales lifecycle so that every manual action a salesperson takes is recorded as a time-stamped state in the CRM, with the external quote number carried across
Specified the data elements per stage and what is automated versus manual — the distinction that drives the estimate
Interrogated 'real time' against actual API constraints instead of accepting the phrase
Wrote the discovery question set, ran it through the client, and treated their pushback questions as findings rather than noise
Produced process flows for lead qualification, deal and quotation, application data flow, and the source-of-truth responsibility model
Delivered the Initial System Overview through four versions to a partner's document standard
deliverablesProduced: Lead qualification and deal/quotation process flows

effect.statement: Pipeline visibility and a recorded sales history without disturbing two working operational systems, and a written source-of-truth model that removes the most expensive argument a project like this can have. Outcome measures were not published.

title: Training, Assessment and Verification Lifecycle
locationContext: South African early-childhood development sector organisation
executiveSummary: A sector organisation running study and training programmes needed its assessment and verification lifecycle in a CRM. The requirement arrived as eleven working documents, several meeting transcripts, a workshop board and a set of flow proposals that did not entirely agree with each other. The work was to reconcile them into one implementation-ready specification — and to decide what Phase 1 would not include.
industryContext: Training and assessment organisations carry a structural complexity that a sales-oriented CRM does not anticipate: a participant is assessed by an assessor, against competencies, at an event, resulting in a verification outcome that may be partial. Those are not deals, and pretending they are produces a system nobody uses.
businessModel: Study and training programmes delivered to participants in the early-childhood development sector, with assessors, trainers and partner organisations involved in delivery and verification.
operationalChallenge: Two distinct request types — study and training — following different lifecycles through the same intake
A training event serving multiple training requests simultaneously
Assessors, trainers, competencies and skills needing structured records that no standard module provided
Verification outcomes that could be partial rather than pass/fail
Web forms writing into the CRM against existing records
Source material spread across working documents, transcripts and workshop boards, with contradictions between them
A client-facing specification that could not expose any of the internal reconciliation work

currentState.summary: The process existed and worked, distributed across people, spreadsheets and a learning platform. No single system held the relationship between a participant, an assessor, an event and a verification outcome.
steps: Participants and assessors matched by hand
risks: Assessment and verification history not queryable
Event capacity and demand not linked
Assessor skills matched from memory
Partial outcomes with no consistent treatment
gaps: No structured assessor or trainer profile
No link between an event and the requests it serves
Form submissions not writing to records

processGaps: The source material described the same process differently in different places, and nobody had reconciled them
Study and training requests were being modelled together despite different lifecycles
The relationship between one training event and many training requests had no representation
Verification could be partial, and no design accounted for that
Trainer availability and publishing were assumed automatic; they were not
Form submissions carrying record identifiers had no validation step, which is an integrity problem, not a convenience one
keyDiscoveryQuestions: Are study and training the same process with a different label, or genuinely different lifecycles?
Can one event serve several requests, and if so, what does that do to the data model?
What is the final positive state for each lifecycle — what does 'finished well' mean?
Can a verification be partial, and if so, what happens next?
What does a form submission need to know about an existing record before it writes to it?
Which of these requirements is Phase 1, and which is the ambition?

futureState.summary: One intake with two typed lifecycles, custom modules for the things a sales CRM cannot express, and blueprint-driven state transitions with defined final states — plus an explicit exclusion list for Phase 1.
steps: Request typed as study or training
Study lifecycle or training lifecycle followed via its own blueprint
Training event created and linked to multiple training requests
Participants and assessors matched against structured skills
Final positive state reached and reported
controls: Deal type determines the blueprint, not the user
Defined final positive state per lifecycle
Structured competency and skill records rather than free text
Form-submitted record identifiers validated before write
Manual steps documented as manual rather than assumed automatic

solutionArchitecture.summary: Standard CRM objects for intake and request handling, plus a set of custom modules for the domain concepts that no standard object represents: events, assessor profiles, competencies, skills, the assessor-skill link, and verification itself.

layers.name: Intake layer
layers.role: Leads, qualification, and typed requests routed into the correct lifecycle

layers.name: Request layer
layers.role: Study and training requests as typed records, each with its own blueprint and final state

layers.name: Domain modules
layers.role: Training event, participant-assessor, verification, competency, assessor profile, skills, assessor-skill link, trainer profile

layers.name: Form layer
layers.role: Public and internal forms writing into the CRM with validated record identifiers

layers.name: Excluded from Phase 1
layers.role: Assessor, trainer and partner portals; external data-sync APIs; replacement of the existing learning platform

dataFlow: Enquiry → lead → qualification → typed request
Training request → linked to a training event (many requests to one event)
Event → participant-assessor records → competency assessment → verification outcome
Assessor profile ↔ skills, via an explicit link record rather than a multi-select field
Form submission → identifier validated → record created or updated

standardVsCustom.reasoning: Modelled as typed records on a standard object rather than as separate custom modules. A separate study module was considered and rejected — it would have doubled the maintenance for a difference that a type field expresses.

standardVsCustom.requirement: Training event, verification, competency, assessor and trainer profiles
standardVsCustom.reasoning: These are the domain. No standard object represents an assessor's skill set or a partial verification outcome, and forcing them into one would have made the system a filing cabinet.
A link record rather than a multi-select, because the relationship carries its own data and needs to be queryable from both sides.
Blueprint functionality handled this natively once the state model was agreed.
Kept manual in Phase 1 by decision. Automating it depended on systems outside the phase, and the honest thing was to document it as manual rather than imply otherwise.

scopeDecisions.reasoning: Each portal is an external-user surface with its own permission model and support burden. Phase 1 needed the internal lifecycle to be right first.

scopeDecisions.requirement: External data-sync APIs and learning-platform replacement
scopeDecisions.reasoning: Large, separable, and dependent on decisions the organisation had not made.

scopeDecisions.requirement: A separate custom module for study requests
scopeDecisions.decision: Rejected in favour of a typed record
scopeDecisions.reasoning: The lifecycles differ; the object does not. Type plus blueprint expresses it with a fraction of the maintenance.

scopeDecisions.decision: Manual in Phase 1, documented as such
scopeDecisions.reasoning: An assumed automation that does not exist is worse than an acknowledged manual step.

dependencies: Client decisions on open lifecycle options before build
Form platform access and identifier strategy
clientResponsibilities: Resolve the open lifecycle decisions rather than deferring them into build
Confirm the skill and competency taxonomy
Provide business users for UAT who run the process, not only manage it
Own the manual steps Phase 1 leaves manual
risks: Source material contradictions resurfacing as change requests during build
Partial verification treatment expanding once real cases arrive
Form identifier tampering if validation is treated as optional
Portals being reintroduced by increment
A client-facing document leaking internal reasoning and undermining confidence
contribution: Reconciled eleven working documents, meeting transcripts, workshop boards and flow proposals into one coherent specification
Audited the specification against the source material and against six implementation scenarios, then applied the findings silently rather than narrating them to the client
Established the study-versus-training modelling decision and the one-event-to-many-requests relationship
Defined the custom module set and justified each one against a standard alternative
Defined the final positive state for each lifecycle — the question that turns a flow into a specification
Insisted on identifier validation for form submissions as an integrity requirement
Set the flowchart standard (state, transition, negative transition, final positive state) and produced the lifecycle diagrams to it
Enforced the client-facing discipline: no change logs, no internal provenance, no transcript citations, no named individuals — the document reads as a specification, not as evidence of effort
deliverablesProduced: Technical Requirement Document (iterated through audit cycles)
Five lifecycle flowcharts to a defined visual standard
Gap and alignment audit against source material

effect.statement: One implementation-ready specification in place of eleven partially-agreeing sources, with a Phase 1 boundary the organisation had explicitly chosen. Outcome measures were not published.

title: Retail Customer-Support Transformation
executiveSummary: A retail business was handling customer enquiries across messaging, social media, email and phone, with returns, exchanges, damage and defects all treated as the same kind of problem. The work was to separate the outcomes, define what 'resolved' means, and build a single support process that survives four channels.
industryContext: Retail support is where the whole operation shows its cracks: a support conversation is really an order problem, a stock problem or a logistics problem wearing a different hat. Designing the support process without touching the order and returns processes produces a faster way to fail.
businessModel: Multi-channel retail selling directly to consumers, with support arriving on whichever channel the customer already uses.
operationalChallenge: Enquiries arriving on messaging, social, email and phone with only some becoming records
The same customer handled by several agents who could not see each other's replies
Returns, exchanges, refunds, defects and transit damage all called 'a return'
No definition of resolved, so tickets closed early and reopened
Customers chasing because nobody told them anything
Priority set by whoever complained loudest

currentState.summary: Support happened wherever the customer put it. Some of it became a ticket. The rest lived in a channel inbox, a personal phone, or a colleague's memory.
risks: No history against the customer
Inconsistent outcomes for identical problems
Courier and supplier claim windows missed
Stock not adjusted on returns
No basis for measuring anything

processGaps: Four channels, one of which was somebody's personal phone, and no single record
Defect, transit damage and misuse were resolved identically despite the money being entirely different
Refunds were issued before inspection, or long after, depending on the agent
Evidence was requested inconsistently, so courier and supplier claims failed later
Categories had grown organically until reporting was meaningless
keyDiscoveryQuestions: Every way a customer can reach you — including the ones you wish they did not use?
What are the genuinely different failure types, and does the money differ per type?
When is the customer refunded — on request, on collection, or on inspection?
What does 'resolved' mean here, and who says so?
How long do you have to claim against a courier or a supplier?
What are the top five things customers contact you about, and why do they keep happening?

futureState.summary: Every channel creates a ticket. Classification decides the path. Return, exchange, refund, repair, transit damage and defect are separate outcomes with separate evidence requirements and separate liability.
steps: Enquiry captured from any channel as a ticket
Evidence collected where liability may be claimed
Outcome executed — refund, exchange, repair, reject
Recovery claim raised against courier or supplier
Closed with category and root cause
controls: Every channel creates a ticket
Mandatory classification before assignment
SLA per priority with business hours
Evidence requirements per failure type
Inspection before refund
Mandatory closure category and root cause

solutionArchitecture.summary: A helpdesk as the single support surface, integrated to the channels customers actually use, with links out to the order, stock and finance systems rather than copies of them.

layers.name: Helpdesk
layers.role: Ticket capture, classification, assignment, SLA, resolution and history

layers.name: Channel integrations
layers.role: Messaging, social, email and telephony feeding tickets rather than inboxes

layers.name: Commerce / order system
layers.role: Order and item reference, remains the source of truth for the order

layers.name: Inventory
layers.role: Stock disposition on returns — resaleable, damaged, write-off

layers.name: Finance
layers.role: Refunds and credit notes, executed where the ledger lives

dataFlow: Channel message → ticket with customer and order match
Classification → path (query, return, exchange, defect, transit damage, complaint)
Return authorised → goods received → inspection outcome → stock disposition
Outcome → refund or replacement executed in the system that owns it
Liability determined → recovery claim raised against courier or supplier
Closure category and root cause → trend reporting

standardVsCustom.reasoning: Core helpdesk functionality. Building any of it would have been indefensible.
Dependent on third-party business-messaging approval, which is a timeline risk outside the project's control and had to be flagged as a dependency, not a task.
Distinct ticket types with their own required fields and evidence rules — configuration, not code.
Claim windows, evidence and recovery against couriers and suppliers had no standard representation, and the money involved justified the build.
Inventory owns stock. The helpdesk records the decision and the inventory system records the movement.

scopeDecisions.requirement: Agents continuing to answer on the channel directly
scopeDecisions.decision: Designed out, with a transition plan
scopeDecisions.reasoning: The system cannot beat a habit that is faster. The channel had to route into the helpdesk so the fast path and the recorded path were the same path.
The existing categories had grown until reporting meant nothing. A smaller set with a defined owner was worth more than a complete one.

scopeDecisions.decision: Mandatory on complaints and failures only
scopeDecisions.reasoning: Mandating it everywhere guarantees it becomes noise.

dependencies: Courier claim window and evidence requirements
clientResponsibilities: Confirm the definition of resolved
Set refund authority limits
Retire the personal-phone channel
Provide agents for UAT
Own the category taxonomy after go-live
risks: Channel approval delaying go-live regardless of build readiness
Agents reverting to the channel because it is faster
Category proliferation returning within a year
Evidence collection failing at the customer, which is where every claim starts
Refund timing conflicting between customer expectation and finance policy
contribution: Separated one 'returns' process into distinct outcomes with distinct liability and distinct evidence
Designed the channel-to-ticket capture so the recorded path is also the fast path
Defined resolution, and made someone accountable for saying so
Built the priority and SLA matrix from what the business had actually promised, not from what sounded reasonable
Introduced evidence requirements tied to claim windows, so recovery stops failing silently
Rebuilt the category taxonomy to a size that produces usable trend data
Structured UAT around real scenarios from the operation rather than a happy-path demo
deliverablesProduced: Current and future-state support process flows

effect.statement: One support process across four channels, distinct outcomes for distinct failures, and trend data the business can act on. Outcome measures were not published.

title: Field Service: Three Job Types, One System
executiveSummary: An installation business needed field service, CRM, accounting and support working as one operation. The design decision that mattered was refusing to force three genuinely different job types down one workflow — and the delivery lesson was that address validation, deposit rules and skill-based scheduling are never as small as they look at scoping.
industryContext: Installation work is not a ticket and not a project — it is somewhere between. The job has a quote, a deposit, a scheduled visit, a skilled resource, parts, a customer standing in their own home, and an invoice that should not wait for paperwork to come back from a van.
businessModel: Installation and home-improvement services sold to consumers, delivered by field teams, with jobs varying from standard fits to bespoke work and larger multi-visit projects.
operationalChallenge: Three job types with genuinely different workflows being described as one process
Scheduling done by whiteboard and phone, with skills matched from memory
Deposits calculated by rules that lived in someone's head
Job completion evidenced by photographs in a messaging group
Invoicing waiting for paperwork to return from site
Support, sales, job and accounting data sitting in four places

currentState.summary: A working business held together by coordination effort. Every job type flowed through the same informal path, and the differences between them were absorbed by people rather than expressed by the process.
risks: Wrong technician or missing parts on site
Deposit gate applied inconsistently
Invoice delay
No first-time-fix data
Customer expectations set by whoever answered the phone
gaps: No job-type distinction in the workflow
No link from job to invoice

processGaps: Standard, custom and project work were being forced through one blueprint, so exceptions were the norm
Deposit calculation differed per job type and was undocumented
Address quality was assumed, and failed dispatches were treated as bad luck
Skill requirements existed but only in the scheduler's head
Completion evidence had no home, so invoicing was blocked by logistics rather than by work
keyDiscoveryQuestions: What are the genuinely different job types, and how does the work differ?
What has to be true before a technician is dispatched?
How is the deposit calculated — and are there exceptions?
What is captured on site today, and on what device?
What triggers the invoice?
Does the technician have signal where the work happens?

futureState.summary: Job type determines the blueprint. Skills and parts are checked before dispatch. Completion evidence is captured on site and releases the invoice.
steps: Deposit calculated by rule and taken
Requirements assessed — skills, parts, access, duration
Work performed and recorded on site
controls: Job type drives the workflow, not a generic path
Deposit gate before scheduling
Skill and part checks before dispatch
Address validated before scheduling
Mandatory completion evidence
Invoice triggered by completion, not by paperwork

solutionArchitecture.summary: Field service as the operational core, CRM for the customer relationship, accounting for money and a helpdesk for post-installation support — with the job as the record that connects them.

layers.name: Field service
layers.role: Job lifecycle, scheduling, dispatch, on-site capture, completion

layers.name: CRM
layers.role: Customer, enquiry, quotation and relationship history

layers.name: Accounting
layers.role: Deposits, invoices and payments — the financial source of truth

layers.name: Helpdesk
layers.role: Post-installation support tied to the job and the asset

dataFlow: Enquiry → quote → deposit invoice raised in accounting
Deposit confirmed → job created with type → blueprint applied
Job requirements → scheduling against skills and availability
Dispatch → on-site capture of time, parts and evidence → completion
Completion → invoice raised in accounting → payment recorded
Post-installation issue → helpdesk ticket linked to the job

standardVsCustom.reasoning: Core field-service functionality.
Separate blueprints per job type. Forcing one workflow would have been faster to build and wrong from the first bespoke job.
Rules varied by job type and value in ways standard functionality did not express. Contained, but real — and larger than the original scope allowed for.
Failed dispatches trace back to address quality. Underestimated at scoping and reported as a scope deviation rather than absorbed.
The skill model had to exist before matching could happen, which made this a data project as well as a scheduling one.

scopeDecisions.requirement: One blueprint for all job types
scopeDecisions.reasoning: The three job types differ in the work, not the label. One blueprint would have produced permanent workarounds.

scopeDecisions.requirement: Deposit rules, address validation and skill-based scheduling
scopeDecisions.decision: Recognised as additional effort and reported
scopeDecisions.reasoning: Each had been scoped as a small item and was not. Reporting the deviation against the baseline is the alternative to absorbing it silently and losing the estimate.
The requirement moved. Documenting it as a change rather than a defect kept the baseline meaningful.

dependencies: Mobile device and connectivity reality on site
clientResponsibilities: Define the skill taxonomy
Confirm deposit rules per job type in writing
Provide technicians for UAT, not only office staff
Accept that completion evidence is mandatory
risks: Connectivity on site making mobile capture unreliable
Technicians reverting to the messaging group
Skill data being incomplete at go-live
Scope absorbed as goodwill until the margin disappears
contribution: Established that three job types needed three blueprints, and defended it against the simpler wrong answer
Documented the blueprint workflows for each job type across successive user-manual versions
Produced the scope deviation and additional effort report covering address validation, deposit calculation, skill-based scheduling and blueprint rework — so the extra work was decided, not absorbed
Designed the completion-evidence gate that releases invoicing from paperwork
Structured UAT around real job types with the technicians who would run them
Produced the user documentation to the partner's brand standard
deliverablesProduced: Scope deviation and additional effort report

effect.statement: One operation across field service, CRM, accounting and support, with job types that behave like the different work they are — and a scope baseline that stayed honest under pressure. Outcome measures were not published.

title: Claims Process Rebuild with Cascading Classification
executiveSummary: A flooring supply and installation business needed its claims process rebuilt across CRM, a custom application, forms and document storage. Five separate source materials described the process, none of them the same way. The core design problem was a three-level cascading classification — product type, claim type, issue type — that determined everything downstream, and which had been treated as a dropdown.
industryContext: Flooring and installation claims sit between retail support, manufacturing defect and installation quality. The same customer complaint can be a product fault, an installation error or a site condition, and the correct process, the evidence required and the party who pays are different in each case.
businessModel: A flooring supply and installation business selling and installing flooring and related products, with claims arriving from customers and requiring inspection before liability can be established.
operationalChallenge: Product type, claim type and issue type interact in a cascading dependency that drives the entire downstream process
Inspectors are involved in the workflow but sit partly outside the organisation
Five source materials described the process differently and had never been reconciled
Evidence and inspection outcomes lived in email and documents
Approval and liability decisions had no consistent structure

currentState.summary: A claims process that worked through people and email, with classification decided case by case and outcomes recorded inconsistently.
risks: Inconsistent liability decisions
Evidence lost
Supplier recovery not pursued
No trend data on failure causes
Inspector engagement dependent on individuals
gaps: No evidence requirements per claim type
No link between claim, product and installation

processGaps: The cascading dependency between product type, claim type and issue type was being handled as three unrelated dropdowns, which means every combination is possible — including the impossible ones
Inspector participation had no defined access path, so a workflow step depended on someone outside the system
Evidence requirements varied by claim type but were not encoded, so claims failed later for missing evidence
Approval authority for remedies was undefined
The five source materials contained genuine contradictions that a build would have inherited
keyDiscoveryQuestions: Which combinations of product, claim and issue type are actually possible — and which are nonsense?
Who inspects, and are they internal, contracted or independent?
What evidence must exist before liability can be determined?
Who is allowed to approve a remedy, and up to what value?
When these five documents disagree, which one is right?
What happens to a claim where the inspector is unavailable?

futureState.summary: Classification as a controlled cascading structure held as data, driving the evidence requirements, the inspection path and the approval route.
steps: Claim logged against the customer, product and installation
Claim type filtered by product type
Issue type filtered by claim type
Inspection triggered where the classification requires it
controls: Cascading classification held as configurable data, not code
Evidence requirements per classification combination
Inspection as a gated step where required
Approval authority by remedy value
Mandatory root cause before closure

solutionArchitecture.summary: CRM for the customer and claim record, a custom application for the classification and inspection structure, forms for structured capture from parties outside the CRM, and document storage for evidence.

layers.name: CRM
layers.role: Customer, claim record, communication and history

layers.name: Custom application
layers.role: Cascading classification model, inspection records, evidence requirements and liability decisions

layers.name: Forms
layers.role: Structured capture from inspectors and other parties without full system access

layers.name: Document storage
layers.role: Evidence, inspection reports and supporting documentation

dataFlow: Claim raised in CRM against customer, product and installation
Classification cascade applied → evidence requirements determined
Inspection required → form issued to inspector → findings captured back against the claim
Evidence stored and linked to the claim record
Liability determined → approval routed → remedy executed
Recovery raised against supplier or installer → root cause logged

standardVsCustom.reasoning: A dependency table held as data. Hard-coding the combinations would have made every business change a development request, and the combinations change.
Standard CRM objects were sufficient once classification was modelled properly.
Structured forms rather than system licences. Inspectors need to contribute, not to browse — and giving external parties full access is a permission problem nobody wants.
Document storage linked to the claim, with retention handled where retention belongs.

scopeDecisions.requirement: Inspector portal with full system access
scopeDecisions.reasoning: Lower cost, lower risk, and it matches what inspectors actually do — submit findings, not manage records.

scopeDecisions.decision: Blocked pending a client-supplied dependency table
scopeDecisions.reasoning: The model cannot be built from guesses. This was raised as an open item rather than assumed, because a wrong cascade is invisible until it is expensive.

scopeDecisions.requirement: Additional workflow requirements identified during reconciliation
scopeDecisions.reasoning: Several requirements existed in one source and not the others. Recording them as open rather than resolving them unilaterally kept the specification honest.

dependencies: Client-supplied cascading dependency table for product, claim and issue type
Confirmation of specific workflow requirements identified as gaps
clientResponsibilities: Supply the classification dependency table
Resolve the contradictions between their own source materials
Confirm approval authority limits
Confirm the inspector engagement model
risks: Building the cascade before the dependency table exists guarantees rework
Contradictions between source materials resurfacing as change requests
Inspector availability constraining the process regardless of system design
Evidence requirements being relaxed in practice and claims failing at recovery
contribution: Reconciled five source materials into one Technical Requirement Document, recording contradictions as open items rather than silently choosing
Identified the cascading classification as the structural centre of the process rather than a UI detail
Specified it as a configurable dependency table so the business can change combinations without a developer
Designed inspector participation through structured forms rather than system access
Tied evidence requirements to the classification combination so recovery stops failing on missing proof
Raised the unresolved dependency table and specific workflow gaps as blockers rather than assumptions
Drafted the client-facing communication to the agreed format
deliverablesProduced: Technical Requirement Document reconciling five source materials

effect.statement: A claims process where classification drives evidence, inspection and liability by rule rather than by case-by-case judgement — with the unresolved dependencies visible as blockers before build rather than after. Outcome measures were not published.

title: Non-profit Operational Application
executiveSummary: A non-profit rescue organisation needed to manage volunteers, training, incidents, assets, vehicles and donors — none of which is a sales pipeline. The work was to recognise that a standard CRM would only carry part of it, and to design a custom operational application for the rest without building a second CRM by accident.
industryContext: Non-profits are frequently sold CRM as a donor tool and then asked to run their entire operation in it. The operational side — volunteers, equipment, vehicles, incidents, training currency — has more in common with field operations and asset management than with fundraising.
businessModel: A volunteer-based rescue organisation funded by donations, operating equipment and vehicles, responding to incidents, and maintaining trained and current volunteers.
operationalChallenge: Volunteers, not employees — availability, training currency and role qualification all matter and none are HR records
Assets and vehicles needing custody, maintenance and compliance tracking
Incidents requiring records that link people, equipment and outcome
Training currency determining who may do what
Donors needing relationship management that is genuinely CRM work
Limited budget and limited administrative capacity, which is a design constraint

currentState.summary: Spreadsheets and institutional knowledge, held together by a small number of committed people who each hold part of the picture.
risks: Training currency unknown at the moment it matters
Equipment custody untracked
Vehicle compliance dates missed
Incident records incomplete
Key-person dependency across the whole operation
gaps: No volunteer record with qualification currency
No structured link between donor and operational activity

processGaps: Training currency was known by the training officer, not by the system, at exactly the moment it needed to be known by a coordinator
Equipment custody was informal, so 'where is it' had no answer
Vehicle compliance was tracked in calendar reminders
Incidents were reconstructed after the fact rather than recorded during
The donor relationship and the operational reality had no connection, which is a fundraising loss as well as an admin one
keyDiscoveryQuestions: What does a volunteer need to be current in before they can attend an incident?
Who is allowed to issue equipment, and does anyone record it?
What must be true for this vehicle to be legal to drive today?
What do you need to be able to say about an incident afterwards, and to whom?
Who administers this system when it is live — and how many hours do they have?

futureState.summary: A custom operational application for volunteers, training, incidents, assets and vehicles, with CRM used for the donor relationship — each doing what it is actually for.
steps: Volunteer registered with role and qualifications
Incident logged with people, equipment and vehicles involved
Equipment issued and returned as custody events
Vehicle compliance monitored with advance alerts
controls: Training currency as a gate, not a report
Custody transfer as a recorded event
Compliance dates with lead-time alerts
Role-based access appropriate to a volunteer organisation
Incident record completeness before closure

solutionArchitecture.summary: A custom application for the operational domain, CRM for donors, and a deliberately small administrative surface — because the organisation's real constraint is administrative capacity, not licence cost.

layers.name: Custom operational application
layers.role: Volunteers, training currency, incidents, assets, vehicles, custody

layers.name: CRM
layers.role: Donors and supporter relationships

layers.name: Reporting
layers.role: Operational and governance reporting for the committee

layers.name: Access layer
layers.role: Role-based permissions appropriate to volunteers rather than employees

dataFlow: Volunteer record → training records → currency status
Incident → linked volunteers (currency-checked), equipment and vehicles
Equipment issue → custody event → return event
Vehicle compliance dates → advance alerts → renewal
Operational activity → committee reporting
Donor records → CRM, separate from operational data

standardVsCustom.requirement: Volunteers, training currency, incidents, assets, vehicles
standardVsCustom.reasoning: None of these is a lead, deal or contact. Forcing them into CRM objects would have produced a system that technically holds the data and answers no question anyone asks.
This genuinely is CRM work and needed nothing custom.
Date-driven alerts are standard. The value was in getting the dates into a system at all.
Volunteers are not employees and the access model has to reflect that — configuration, but thought about deliberately.

scopeDecisions.reasoning: Cheaper on paper. It would have produced a donor system with operational data hidden in custom fields, which is the outcome this organisation was already living with in spreadsheets.

scopeDecisions.decision: Treated as a hard design constraint
scopeDecisions.reasoning: A volunteer organisation has no spare administrator. A design that needs one has failed regardless of how good it is.
Different audiences, different access, different purposes. Merging them creates a privacy problem and a usability one.

clientResponsibilities: Define qualification currency rules
Cleanse the asset and vehicle registers
Name a system owner with realistic hours
Accept a smaller Phase 1 than the wish list
risks: Administrative capacity below what any system needs
Register data quality at go-live
Volunteer adoption where the alternative is a familiar spreadsheet
Scope growing to match ambition rather than capacity
contribution: Established that the operational domain needed a custom application and the donor domain did not
Modelled training currency as a gate on incident participation rather than a report nobody opens
Designed custody as an event so equipment location has an answer
Treated administrative capacity as a design constraint rather than an implementation detail
Kept donor and operational data separate for access and privacy reasons
Phased the scope to what the organisation could actually run
deliverablesProduced: Process flows for volunteer, incident, asset and vehicle management

effect.statement: An operational system that answers the questions the organisation actually asks — who is current, where is the equipment, is the vehicle legal — with donor management kept where it belongs. Outcome measures were not published.

title: Webhook-to-Helpdesk Integration
executiveSummary: An external platform needed to raise support tickets automatically when defined events occurred. Technically small. The design work was everything around it: what happens when the payload is wrong, what happens when the same event fires twice, and what a ticket created by a machine at 3am is supposed to do next.
industryContext: Event-driven integration is usually scoped as a connection. The connection is the easy part. The process design — deduplication, failure handling, ownership of a machine-created record — is where these projects either work or quietly produce a queue nobody trusts.
businessModel: A partner-led engagement supporting an external platform that needed operational events to become actionable support records.
operationalChallenge: External platform events needing to create tickets automatically
Payloads varying in completeness
Duplicate events firing for the same underlying incident
No defined owner for a ticket nobody raised
Failures needing to be visible rather than silent

currentState.summary: Events happened on the platform and reached the support team by whatever route someone had improvised — or did not reach them at all.
steps: Ticket raised manually if it seems important
risks: Events missed entirely
Response depending on someone watching
No audit trail from event to resolution
Duplicate effort on the same incident
gaps: No ownership rule for automated tickets

processGaps: The integration was described as 'send a webhook and create a ticket', which omits every decision that matters
Duplicate events had no treatment, so one incident could become five tickets
A malformed or incomplete payload had no defined outcome — silently dropping it is the default and the worst option
An automatically-created ticket had no owner, no priority rule and no SLA
There was no way to tell whether the integration had stopped working
keyDiscoveryQuestions: What event, exactly, should create a ticket — and what should not?
What is the minimum payload for a ticket to be actionable?
If the same event fires twice, is that two tickets or one?
Who owns a ticket that no human raised?
What happens when the payload is malformed — and who finds out?
How would you know if this integration silently stopped?

futureState.summary: Defined events, validated payloads, conditional ticket creation with deduplication, explicit ownership and visible failure handling.
steps: Event occurs on the external platform
Payload validated against the required contract
Conditional logic determines whether a ticket is warranted
Ticket created with classification, priority and owner
Support handles as a normal ticket
controls: Explicit event contract
Payload validation before creation
Deduplication window
Assignment and priority rules for machine-created tickets
Error logging with alerting
Health visibility

solutionArchitecture.summary: A webhook endpoint with validation and conditional logic in front of the helpdesk, so the helpdesk receives decisions rather than raw events.

layers.name: External platform
layers.role: Event source. Outside the project's control.

layers.name: Integration layer
layers.role: Endpoint, payload validation, deduplication, conditional logic, error handling and logging

layers.name: Helpdesk
layers.role: Ticket creation, classification, assignment, SLA and resolution

layers.name: Monitoring
layers.role: Error visibility and integration health

dataFlow: Platform event → webhook → endpoint
Payload validated → invalid payloads logged and surfaced, never silently dropped
Deduplication check → duplicate suppressed or appended to the existing ticket
Conditional rules → ticket created with classification, priority and owner
Ticket → normal support process
Errors → log → alert

standardVsCustom.reasoning: Helpdesk functionality. Nothing to build.
The contract with the external platform is specific, and validating it is the difference between a useful queue and a noisy one.
The definition of a duplicate is a business rule, not a technical default. It had to be decided before it could be built.
Silent failure is the standard behaviour of an unmonitored integration, and it is the failure mode that costs the most trust.

scopeDecisions.reasoning: Not the project's system. The integration must tolerate what the platform sends, including what it sends badly.
Whether a failed event is retried, dropped or replayed is a decision with operational consequences and belongs in the specification.
The integration creates the record. Deciding what to do about it remains a human job.

dependencies: Event contract and payload specification from the external platform
Deduplication rule confirmed by the business
clientResponsibilities: Define which events warrant a ticket
Confirm the deduplication rule
Name the owner for machine-created tickets
Nominate who receives error alerts
risks: Payload contract changing without notice on the external side
Duplicate storms creating ticket noise and destroying trust in the queue
Silent failure going unnoticed
Automated tickets with no owner accumulating
contribution: Turned 'send a webhook, create a ticket' into a specification with an event contract, validation, deduplication and failure handling
Established the deduplication rule as a business decision rather than a technical default
Insisted on visible error handling — a silently failing integration is worse than none
Defined ownership, classification and SLA for tickets nobody raised
Kept automated resolution out of scope and said why

effect.statement: Platform events becoming actionable tickets with owners and SLAs, and an integration whose failures are visible rather than silent. Outcome measures were not published.

title: Manufacturing Quality and Tooling Process
locationContext: Manufacturing business with an internal tool room
executiveSummary: A manufacturer needed its quality-control and tool-room processes structured: forms, agreements, trial cycles and participation from departments and suppliers outside the core team. The design constraint was that the shop floor's paper forms were fast, and any replacement that was slower would simply be ignored.
industryContext: Manufacturing quality is iterative, not linear. First articles, trials, adjustments and re-inspection loop until the part is right — a workflow shaped for a sales pipeline cannot express that, and neither can a form that assumes one submission.
businessModel: A manufacturing operation with an internal tool room, external departments and suppliers participating in quality and trial cycles.
operationalChallenge: Quality checks captured on paper and filed
Trial and first-article cycles run informally and iteratively
External departments and suppliers participating without system access
Agreements and forms living outside any process
Non-conformance recorded but root cause and corrective action not linked
Tool-room work coordinated verbally

currentState.summary: A working quality culture with no system underneath it. The knowledge existed; the data did not.
risks: Repeat root causes invisible
Corrective actions closed without verification
Supplier quality trends unknown
Trial history not retained
Paper lost

processGaps: Inspection results were recorded but not queryable, so the same root cause recurred without anyone being able to prove it
Trial cycles are iterative and were being described as a linear approval
External departments and suppliers were part of the process but had no way in
Corrective actions were closed rather than verified
The paper form was fast, and no one had asked why it had survived
keyDiscoveryQuestions: Where do you find problems today, and where should you?
What is recorded on paper, and why has that survived every previous system?
How many trial cycles does a difficult part take, and what changes between them?
Who outside your company takes part in this process, and how do they contribute today?
Is a corrective action ever verified, or just closed?
Where does material go when it fails?

futureState.summary: Defined inspection points with structured capture, non-conformance with mandatory root cause, iterative trial cycles modelled as cycles, and controlled participation for external parties through forms.
steps: Quality criteria defined per item and stage
controls: Defined inspection points
Mandatory evidence
Segregation of failed material
No closure without root cause and verification
Approval on conditional release
Trial cycles recorded as iterations, not overwrites

solutionArchitecture.summary: A custom application for the quality and tooling domain, with structured forms as the participation path for parties outside it, and capture designed to be at least as fast as the paper it replaces.

layers.name: Quality application
layers.role: Criteria, inspections, non-conformance, corrective actions, trial cycles

layers.name: Forms
layers.role: Shop-floor and external capture, designed for speed

layers.name: Asset / tooling records
layers.role: Tools and their history

layers.name: Reporting
layers.role: Trends by part, batch, supplier and root cause

dataFlow: Inspection point reached → structured capture with evidence
Fail → non-conformance raised → material segregated
Root cause investigation → corrective action assigned to an owner
Tool-room adjustment → trial cycle → re-inspection → iteration recorded
Verification → closure
Closed records → trend reporting by part, batch, supplier and cause

standardVsCustom.reasoning: The iterative shape of a trial cycle has no standard representation. Modelling it as a linear approval would have been faster and would have lost the history that makes the data worth having.
Structured forms rather than system licences, for the same reason as anywhere else: participants need to contribute, not browse.
Standard approval routing was sufficient once the authority was defined.
Reporting was never the problem; structured data was.

scopeDecisions.decision: Only where the digital path is faster
scopeDecisions.reasoning: A slower replacement will be bypassed and the data will be worse than the paper. Speed on the floor was treated as a requirement, not a nice-to-have.

scopeDecisions.requirement: Full system access for suppliers and external departments
scopeDecisions.reasoning: Controlled participation at a fraction of the cost and none of the permission risk.
The business runs iterations. A system that only records the last one is recording the least useful part.

dependencies: Quality criteria per part or stage
Supplier and external department contact model
clientResponsibilities: Define inspection points and criteria
Provide the tooling register
Confirm who may authorise conditional release
Make shop-floor staff available to test capture speed
risks: Digital capture being slower than paper and therefore ignored
External participation depending on parties with no incentive
Root cause defaulting to a convenient answer
Trial cycle complexity growing beyond the model
contribution: Modelled the trial cycle as an iteration rather than a linear approval
Treated shop-floor capture speed as a hard requirement rather than a usability preference
Designed external participation through forms rather than access
Made verification, not closure, the end of a corrective action
Structured the data so trend analysis by part, batch, supplier and root cause becomes possible for the first time

effect.statement: Quality data that supports trend analysis, trial cycles recorded as the iterations they are, and capture the shop floor will actually use. Outcome measures were not published.

title: Partner Scoping Configurator: Auditing a Product Before It Ships
executiveSummary: An implementation partner wanted a self-service configurator that would let prospects assemble an implementation package and see a price. The build existed and demonstrated well. The audit found that its entire structuring axis contradicted the partner's own product list, that its pricing matched none of the three figures in circulation, and that internal cost fields were exposed in the public bundle. The value was not in building it — it was in finding those before a prospect did.
industryContext: Partner-led sales tooling sits between marketing and delivery. A configurator is a commercial artefact: it makes a price promise on behalf of a delivery team who were not in the room, and it does that at scale, unsupervised.
businessModel: A regional implementation partner selling packaged implementation work, wanting to capture qualified leads through a self-service estimate tool rather than a first sales call.
operationalChallenge: The configurator was structured on an axis the partner's own product catalogue did not use
Three different retail figures existed for the same package, from three different sources
The rate arithmetic in the tool matched neither the partner's cost model nor either retail formula
Internal-only fields including cost and effort data were exposed in the public client bundle
A reproducible crash existed in one category and had not surfaced during the demo
Effort estimates were missing for the large majority of add-on items
The partner wanted price shown only after lead capture — inverting the tool's flow

currentState.summary: A working, demonstrable application with a clear commercial intent, built ahead of the commercial decisions it depended on.
risks: Quoting a price the partner would not honour
Prospects screenshotting estimates and shopping them
Exposed internal cost data
A crash in a live sales flow
Structuring the offer on an axis the partner had rejected
gaps: No effort data behind most items

processGaps: The product model was undecided — one package or six — and the tool had assumed one
The pricing question was not a bug; it was three stakeholders holding three different numbers and nobody reconciling them
Price was shown before contact capture, which is a commercial decision the tool had made by default
Lead delivery was by email, so nothing reached the CRM and nothing could be measured
The funnel had one URL, so drop-off could not be attributed to a step
keyDiscoveryQuestions: Is this one package or six? Because the tool has already answered.
What is the real retail price — and which of these three figures do you stand behind?
Who supplies the effort estimates for the items that have none?
Should a prospect see a price before you know who they are?
What is a lead here — an email, or a record in your CRM?
How will you know which step people leave at?

futureState.summary: The commercial model decided first, then the tool restructured around it: contact capture before price, leads delivered as CRM records, a URL per step for funnel attribution, and internal data removed from the client bundle.
steps: Qualifying branch — new to the platform or existing user
Lead delivered as a CRM record
controls: Commercial model agreed before build
Price gated behind contact capture
Internal cost and effort fields excluded from the client bundle
One URL per step
Leads as records, not emails
Fixed-price positioning defined by the partner

solutionArchitecture.summary: A multi-step client-side configurator with routed steps for analytics, contact capture ahead of price reveal, and a server-side boundary keeping internal commercial data out of the browser.

layers.name: Configurator front-end
layers.role: Step-routed wizard, selection state, validation, localisation

layers.name: Pricing model
layers.role: Retail prices derived from an agreed commercial model — not from internal cost arithmetic exposed to the client

layers.name: Lead delivery
layers.role: Submission to the partner's CRM as a lead record, with the estimate attached

layers.name: Analytics layer
layers.role: Per-step URLs and tag management for funnel drop-off attribution
