layers.name: Internal data boundary
layers.role: Cost, effort and internal scope data kept server-side or out of the artefact entirely

dataFlow: Prospect journey → step-routed URLs → analytics events per step
Selections → retail price computed from the agreed model
Contact capture → price reveal → thank-you step
Submission → lead record in the partner's CRM with the configuration and estimate
Internal cost and effort data → never sent to the client bundle

standardVsCustom.reasoning: A bespoke commercial front-end. Reasonable to build; the question was never whether, but what it should say.
Must be data the partner can change, and must reflect a commercial decision that had not yet been made. Building logic on an undecided price is building twice.
CRM lead creation is standard. Email-only delivery was a project-team constraint, not a requirement — and it cost the partner measurement.
Tag management and per-step routes. Standard, but only possible if the routing is designed for it.

standardVsCustom.requirement: Internal cost fields in the client bundle
standardVsCustom.reasoning: Not a feature at all — a data-exposure defect. Internal commercial data does not belong in a public artefact under any framing.

scopeDecisions.requirement: Continue building against an undecided package model
scopeDecisions.reasoning: One package or six changes the entire structure. Building either while the question is open guarantees rework, and saying so is more useful than shipping.
The partner's commercial judgement — prospects screenshot an estimate and take it elsewhere. The tool's flow had encoded the opposite assumption without anyone deciding it.

scopeDecisions.decision: Escalated as a contradiction, not resolved unilaterally
scopeDecisions.reasoning: The tool used one axis; the partner's catalogue used another and the partner had rejected the first. That is a commercial decision, not a design preference.

scopeDecisions.decision: Raised as a blocking dependency with a named owner required
scopeDecisions.reasoning: A price with no effort behind it is a guess the delivery team has to honour.

dependencies: Partner decision on the package model
Effort estimates for the outstanding items
clientResponsibilities: Decide the package model
Confirm the retail price and stand behind it
Supply the missing effort data or nominate who will
Own the fixed-price positioning commercially
risks: A public tool quoting a price the delivery team cannot deliver at
Internal cost data reaching prospects or competitors
A reproducible crash in a live sales funnel
Rework if the package model is decided after build
Fixed-price positioning without effort data behind it
contribution: Audited the live application against the partner's own product catalogue and the meeting record, and reported the contradiction rather than building around it
Reconciled three conflicting retail figures and showed that the tool's rate matched none of them
Found and reproduced a crash that had not appeared in the demo
Identified internal cost, effort and scope fields exposed in the public bundle as a commercial data-exposure issue
Reduced the position to three decisions blocking development, each with an owner, rather than a list of findings
Produced the audit report, a standalone context brief for handover, and a post-demo change specification
Audited an implementation prompt against the demo record to check that what was about to be built matched what had actually been agreed
Distinguished the partner's commercial decisions from the project team's technical constraints, and did not let the second masquerade as the first
deliverablesProduced: Audit report reconciling the meeting record, the live application and the product catalogue
Coverage matrix against the partner's implementation item list

effect.statement: Three commercial decisions surfaced as explicit blockers before further development, a data-exposure issue caught before public launch, and a reproducible crash found before it appeared in a live sales funnel. Commercial outcomes were not published.

title: Driving School Portal: Custom Front-End Over an Operational Process
executiveSummary: A driver training business needed a customer-facing portal for enrolment, scheduling and payment. A standard CRM would not have been an appropriate surface for the public, so the work was a custom front-end over a structured operational process — with the delivery lesson sitting in payments, staging discipline and an admin dashboard that had been assumed complete.
industryContext: Driver training is a booking business with a compliance layer: sessions, instructors, vehicles and progression against a required curriculum. The customer is a member of the public who will never be given a system licence, which forces a portal rather than an internal application.
businessModel: A driver training business selling courses and sessions to the public, delivered by instructors against vehicles and scheduled capacity.
operationalChallenge: A public-facing surface for enrolment, purchase and scheduling
Local payment methods alongside international card processing
Administrative capability for staff without exposing internal systems
A staging-to-production path that does not break live customers
An admin dashboard whose completeness had been assumed rather than verified

currentState.summary: A business running on manual enrolment and scheduling, with a portal in late-stage development and a set of gaps that had not been catalogued.
steps: Pays by whatever route is available
risks: Manual enrolment limiting growth
Payment friction losing customers
Scheduling conflicts
Admin capability assumed rather than verified

processGaps: The admin dashboard was treated as done; an audit found several required capabilities missing entirely
Local payment gateway integration was blocked on credentials that had not been requested early enough
Staging and production discipline had not been established, which is a process gap rather than a technical one
Booking capacity and instructor availability had no single source
keyDiscoveryQuestions: Who is the user here — and would you ever give them a licence to your internal system?
How do customers in this market expect to pay?
What does an administrator need to do daily that the dashboard cannot do today?
What is the path from staging to production, and who decides?
Who maintains availability, and how often is it wrong?

futureState.summary: A public portal for enrolment, purchase and booking, an administrative surface built for the people who run the business, and a deployment path that separates staging from live.
steps: Pays via card or local method
Administrators manage capacity, content and records
controls: Public surface separate from internal systems
Payment confirmation before booking is committed
Role-based administrative access
Staging-to-production release discipline
Availability from a maintained source

solutionArchitecture.summary: A custom web application with its own data layer and payment integrations, deployed through a staging environment, with an administrative surface sized to what staff actually do.

layers.name: Public portal
layers.role: Registration, course selection, purchase, booking, progress

layers.name: Admin surface
layers.role: Capacity, content, instructor and record management for staff

layers.name: Data layer
layers.role: Students, courses, sessions, bookings, payments

layers.name: Payment integrations
layers.role: International card processing plus the local method the market expects

layers.name: Deployment
layers.role: Staging environment, migrations and a controlled production release path

dataFlow: Registration → student record
Course or session selection → availability check → provisional booking
Payment → confirmation → booking committed
Session delivery → attendance and progress recorded
Admin actions → capacity, content and records
Staging → verified → production

standardVsCustom.reasoning: The user is a member of the public. Internal systems are not a public surface, and no amount of configuration makes them one.
A payment provider's job. Building any part of it would be indefensible.
Market expectation, provider-dependent — and blocked on credentials, which made it a dependency to raise early rather than a task to schedule.
Audited against what administrators actually do, which turned out to be more than had been built.

scopeDecisions.reasoning: The missing capabilities — including record upload, lookup, status workflow and a booking form — were found by review, not by go-live. That is the cheaper order.
Blocked on client-supplied credentials. Scheduling work that cannot start is how plans lose credibility.
Late-stage projects fail at release, not at build.

dependencies: Local payment provider credentials from the client
clientResponsibilities: Provide payment provider credentials
Confirm course structure and pricing
Maintain availability data
Nominate administrators and test the dashboard against real tasks
risks: Payment integration blocked indefinitely on credentials
Admin gaps surfacing after go-live rather than before
Availability drift if maintenance is manual
Production environment hygiene — a live domain is an operational asset and needs to be treated as one
contribution: Established that a public user base requires a custom portal rather than an internal system with permissions
Audited the admin dashboard against real administrative tasks and produced the gap list before go-live rather than after
Escalated the payment gateway credential dependency as a blocker rather than scheduling around it
Established staging-to-production release discipline as a process requirement
Produced phase roadmap documentation so the remaining work had a shape rather than a backlog

effect.statement: A public enrolment and booking surface backed by a verified administrative capability, with the gaps found by audit rather than by customers. Outcome measures were not published.
